svg.title: MedLynq competitive moat
svg.text: Five compounding layers. The deeper layers can't be skipped — each one only opens after the previous fills up.
1 · Five moat layers · how each one deepens

g.text: Workflow lock-in (Months 0–6)
The shallowest layer. Hospital staff use Pulse daily — Patient folder, query inbox, document repository. Once Priya's day runs on MedLynq, switching cost is months of retraining.
Defends against: Excel sheets, generic case-management tools.
Strength: medium · easily started by competitors · only lasts until they catch up on features.
Buys us 6 months while we build deeper layers.
Labelled query dataset (Months 3–18)
Every query · response · outcome is logged. India has no public dataset of government-scheme query-response pairs. We build the first one as a byproduct of usage.
Defends against: Anyone who tries to build an AI classifier from scratch — they would need 12+ months of hospital relationships before they can collect labels.
Strength: strong · compounds with every query · cannot be bought, only built.
Becomes the foundation for layers 3 and 5.
Fine-tuned classifier + India health-claims corpus (Months 12–24)
Once we have 20k+ labelled cases, we fine-tune a small model that nobody else can replicate without our data. Accuracy ladder: 75% → 88% → 92% → 96%.
Defends against: OpenAI / Anthropic generic models. Big tech can build the algo, not the data.
Strength: very strong · accuracy gap becomes user-visible · "Lynq just understands queries better".
Year-2 differentiation. The moat investors care about.
Network intelligence + two-sided liquidity (Months 18–36)
Once ≥25 hospitals run on Lynq, patterns emerge that no single hospital can see ("SHA UP applies 50% adj in 78% of SG075B cases"). Insurer-side mirror brings TPAs onto the same platform.
Defends against: Any single-sided competitor. New entrants need both sides simultaneously to be useful.
Strength: hardest to replicate · classic network effects · grows with usage.
Switching cost = leaving the network. Real competitive durability.
NHCX FHIR integration + HIS contracts (Months 24–48)
When NHCX becomes mandatory, every hospital needs FHIR-compliant middleware. We have 2 years of edge-case knowledge — they don't. HIS vendors (Practo, Insta) embed Lynq via white-label SDK.
Defends against: Late entrants and big consultancies. Replacing Lynq inside Practo would take 12+ months of migration.
Strength: infrastructure-grade · per-claim economics · scales with NHCX volume.
The "operating system" outcome. ~₹15+ crore ARR run-rate visible.

svg.text: 2 · Who might try to compete · and why they can't catch up

g.text: HIS vendors (Practo, Insta, eHospital)
What they have: Hospital workflow + clinical data inside one system.
What they lack: No labelled query-response data. No scheme-specific classifier. No cross-hospital network. No incentive to specialise in claims (their main game is OPD/IPD ops).
Likely response: License Lynq via white-label SDK (₹20-30/claim) — they become a distribution channel, not a competitor.
Threat: medium · but addressable via partnership.
Time to replicate from scratch: 18–24 months.
Big IT consultancies (TCS, Infosys, Wipro)
What they have: Government relationships, FHIR consulting, big team.
What they lack: Product DNA. They build for insurers, not hospitals — their incentive is aligned with payers (slower claim cycle). They build bespoke per client (no SaaS scale).
Likely response: Win a few RFPs for govt-scale deployments. Won't compete on per-hospital pricing or speed.
Threat: low · different game · they can't move at our cadence.
Time to replicate as a product: 36+ months.
NHA itself (government / NHCX)
What they have: The protocol layer (NHCX FHIR API). They mandate it.
What they lack: They explicitly do not build hospital-side software (their charter is infrastructure, not applications). They publish standards and let the market build on top.
Likely response: Friendly tailwind. Mandate forces every hospital to need our category of software.
Threat: zero · they're our enabler, not competitor.
Same logic as Aadhaar → it created Razorpay / Cred / etc.
New healthtech startups (the real threat)
What they could have: Same ambition, same playbook, more capital.
What they lack today: 12–18 months of data. No hospital relationships. No NHCX edge-case knowledge.
Likely response: By the time they're visible enough to copy, we have 25+ hospitals, layer 3, and layer 4 active.
Threat: real · only mitigation is speed to layers 3–4.
Window of vulnerability: Months 6–18.

svg.text: 3 · Why these moats compound (not just stack)

g.text: Data compounding
More usage → more labels → better classifier → fewer queries for users → more usage.
Each new hospital improves prediction for every other hospital.
Synthetic data factory amplifies labelled examples 20-30x.
Year 2 model accuracy gap (96% vs 75% generic) becomes user-visible — sales cycle collapses.
First mover gets to accuracy ladder v4 first.
Network compounding
More hospitals → more scheme behaviour patterns visible → more value to each existing hospital.
Adding the insurer-side mirror turns it into a two-sided network.
25 hospitals = denial pattern alerts work. 100 hospitals = benchmarking is gold. 500 hospitals = data licensing emerges.
Late entrant has to seed both sides simultaneously. Hard.
Integration compounding
Each HIS integration brings 200+ hospitals at once (white-label channel).
ERP / Tally sync makes the CFO finance team's tooling depend on us.
NHCX FHIR mapping requires 2 years of edge-case knowledge — replicate cost goes up over time.
Switching cost = months of migration per hospital.
Why MedLynq is structurally hard to replicate
Time: Layer 2 (dataset) needs 12+ months of real usage. Layer 4 (network) needs 18+ months. Layer 5 (NHCX) needs 24+ months of standards work. No shortcut.
Trust: Hospitals don't share clinical data lightly. Each relationship took months of negotiation. Re-doing that for a new vendor is more painful than staying.
Two-sidedness: Insurer mirror means both sides choose us. Late entrant has to flip both — almost impossible at this category's margin structure.
Standards proximity: NHCX edge cases (FHIR mapping nuances, scheme-specific quirks) are not documented. We learn them by doing. Late entrants re-learn at full cost.
Net: layers 1–2 are catchable. Layers 3–5 compound. By month 18, the gap is unbridgeable in any reasonable funding scenario.

svg.text: 5 · Where the moat is thinnest (own the risk)

g.text: Window of vulnerability · M6–18: we have workflow lock-in but not yet the dataset depth. A well-funded competitor that ships fast could fragment the market.

Mitigations: (a) Free tier accelerates data acquisition (b) Hospital exclusivity clauses in pilots (c) HIS partnerships block alternative distribution (d) Ship layer 3 features fast — every paid feature is a competitor's hurdle.
After M18: Network + corpus + NHCX integration make us the default. Risk shifts from competition to execution.
